summary:
The Old Copper Complex was a Native American metalworking tradition centered on the Great Lakes region of North America, particularly in present-day Wisconsin and Michigan, active from approximately 4000 to 1500 BCE. Practitioners of this tradition used native copper — naturally occurring deposits of pure metallic copper found near Lake Superior — to produce a wide range of tools and ornaments including spear points, knives, fishhooks, awls, beads, and bracelets through cold hammering and annealing techniques.

The Old Copper Complex represents one of the world's earliest sustained traditions of metal object production, predating Bronze Age metallurgy in the Near East in terms of the cold-working of native metals, though it involved no smelting or alloying. Copper objects from the tradition have been found far from their production sites through burial contexts associated with the Archaic period, indicating extensive trade networks across eastern North America. Despite its technological sophistication, the tradition eventually declined rather than developing further, possibly because native copper supplies were depleted or because subsistence strategies shifted as populations grew.
